$4,000 Budget
Your spending in May reached $9,256.10, with childcare and education ($1,870) and your mortgage ($3,850) acting as your largest financial commitments. Since you are early in June with only five days elapsed, analyzing your May patterns reveals four key employer benefits you should review to optimize your household cash flow.
Dependent care FSA (DCFSA)
Based on your $1,650 payment to Bright Horizons and $220 for Care.com backup care, you are spending $1,870 monthly on childcare. At this rate, your annual childcare costs will significantly exceed the $5,000 IRS limit for a Dependent Care Flexible Spending Account (DCFSA).
Since this involves tax-advantaged accounts and IRS rules, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Tax savings: Contributing the full $5,000 to a DCFSA allows you to pay for these expenses with pre-tax dollars. Given your combined household gross income of approximately $330,000, this could potentially save you over $1,500 annually in federal and FICA taxes.
Enrollment: Check if Salesforce or UCHealth allows for mid-year enrollment due to a "qualifying life event," or ensure you maximize this during the next open enrollment period.
HSA employer contributions
In May, you made a $400 contribution to your HealthEquity HSA. For 2026, the family contribution limit is $8,750.
Employer "seed" money: Many employers provide a lump-sum contribution or a matching incentive for employees enrolled in high-deductible health plans. Review your benefit summaries at Salesforce and UCHealth to see if you are leaving "free money" on the table that could cover your $185.40 in healthcare costs seen in May.
Catch-up rule: If either you or your partner are age 55 or older, you can contribute an additional $1,000 catch-up contribution to your respective HSA.
Backup childcare benefits
You utilized Care.com for backup care in May, costing $220. Many large employers, particularly in the healthcare and tech sectors like yours, provide a specific number of subsidized backup care days per year.
Cost reduction: Salesforce and UCHealth may offer a program where you only pay a small copay (often $10–$25) for backup care rather than the full market rate.
Direct partnerships: Check if your employers have a direct partnership with Bright Horizons that offers priority enrollment or tuition discounts, as this is one of your largest recurring monthly expenses.
Retirement matching targets
Your paystub notes show you are deferring 6% at UCHealth ($300) and 8% at Salesforce ($700).
The "match" baseline: Verify the exact matching formula for both employers. If either employer matches up to a higher percentage (e.g., matching 100% up to 10%), you may want to adjust your contributions to capture the full employer match before allocating funds to other categories like your $300 monthly 529 contribution. 📈
UCHealth specific: Many hospital systems offer 403(b) or 401(a) plans with unique vesting schedules; ensure you understand these timelines to protect your employer-paid benefits.
Would you like to see how maximizing your Dependent Care FSA might specifically impact your monthly take-home pay? 🎯
